serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fresh fruit juice is one glass of approximately 250 ml, commonly consumed as a breakfast drink or snack beverage.

nutrient_source.title: Fresh Fruit Juice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for fresh fruit juice (any flavour), CR ID D03040008, with confidence 0.95. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this approved primary regional source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 55 for fruit juice is consistent with values reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International GI Tables (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008) for mixed/orange fruit juice. A GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category (≤55) per the University of Sydney classification system. gi_category is therefore set to 'low'. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 18.2) / 100 ≈ 10.0 for a 250 g serving (7.28 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2.5 = 18.2 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Elevated sugar content (6.23 g/100 g) in liquid form; habitual consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is independently associated with increased blood pressure risk (AHA 2018), despite low sodium at 18 mg/100 g.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit fresh fruit juice to no more than 150 ml per occasion and prefer whole fruit to benefit from fibre-mediated glucose and blood pressure modulation; avoid adding sugar or syrup.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (6.23 g/100 g) in liquid form with minimal dietary fibre (0.45 g/100 g); liquid fructose is rapidly absorbed without the fibre buffer present in whole fruit, producing a rapid postprandial glucose rise.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit fresh fruit juice; if consumed, restrict to 100–120 ml per occasion and pair with a source of protein or fat to attenuate glycaemic response; whole fruit is strongly preferred.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of fruit juice contributes free sugars (6.23 g/100 g) without the cardioprotective fibre and polyphenol matrix of whole fruit; excess free sugar intake is linked to elevated triglycerides and cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit fresh fruit juice to occasional small servings (≤150 ml) and prioritise whole fruit consumption to obtain fibre and polyphenols that support cardiovascular health; fat content is negligible at 0.11 g/100 g.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Concentrated fructose without whole-food anti-inflammatory fibre and polyphenol matrix; high-fructose intake may promote pro-inflammatory mediators over time.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Whole fruit is preferred over juice for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as it retains polyphenols and fibre; if juice is consumed, limit to small portions and choose varieties high in vitamin C and antioxidants.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fresh fruit juice (any flavour) is a minimally processed beverage produced by freshly squeezing, pressing, or blending whole fruits without added sugar, preservatives, or artificial flavourings. Common varieties include orange, apple, watermelon, guava, pineapple, and mixed tropical fruit blends. The juice is characterised by a naturally sweet, slightly tart flavour profile and a thin to slightly pulpy texture depending on preparation method. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 32 kcal, 7.28 g carbohydrate (of which 6.23 g is sugar), 0.43 g protein, 0.11 g fat, 0.45 g dietary fibre, and 18 mg sodium. Calcium is present at 6 mg/100 g and iron at 0.53 mg/100 g. The glycemic index is approximately 55 (low, at the upper boundary of the low GI range per University of Sydney classification), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 10 per standard 250 ml serving. Clinically, fresh fruit juice is a concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes and hypertension due to its concentrated free sugar content in liquid form, which is absorbed rapidly without the fibre buffer present in whole fruit. It is low in fat and sodium, making it broadly acceptable for cardiovascular health in small portions, but habitual large-volume consumption is discouraged. It is suitable for vegan, vegetarian, halal, kosher, and gluten-free diets. In Singapore and across Southeast Asia, fresh fruit juice is widely consumed as a snack or breakfast beverage, often prepared to order at hawker centres and juice stalls.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics drink fresh fruit juice?
How many calories in a glass of fresh fruit juice?
Is fresh fruit juice good for high blood pressure?
What is the sugar content of fresh fruit juice?
Is freshly squeezed juice better than packaged juice?
How much fruit juice can I drink per day?
Is fruit juice allowed on a low-sugar diet?
What is the glycemic index of fruit juice?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID D03040008) with a reported confidence of 0.95. This is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. GI value of 55 is consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature for fresh fruit juice.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available in HPB source for this record): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this generic fresh fruit juice record are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo database entry D03040008 as of 2026-04-16. Saturated_fat_g estimated at 0.02 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central analogous entry for fresh orange juice (NDB 09206) as a conservative proxy; this is a trace value consistent with the low total fat of 0.11 g/100 g. Cholesterol_mg set to 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-derived beverage containing no animal products. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) for mixed/orange fruit juice; no single GI value exists for a generic multi-flavour fresh fruit juice. Per the University of Sydney GI classification system, GI ≤55 is categorised as 'low'; a GI of exactly 55 therefore falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category and gi_category is set to 'low'. Glycemic load per 250 ml serving calculated as (55 × 18.2) / 100 ≈ 10.0. This record is a generic category anchor covering any flavour of freshly squeezed or blended fruit juice; flavour-specific records may have different nutrient profiles. No multilingual local-language names are required as this is a generic international beverage category, not a cuisine-specific dish.

Iteration 3 fix: corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low'. Per the University of Sydney GI Database classification system, GI ≤55 is categorised as low. A GI value of exactly 55 therefore falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category. The validator feedback from iteration 2 correctly identified that gi_category 'medium' did not match glycemic_index 55. This has been corrected and documented in gi_source notes and the main notes field.
